text: The surgeon marks the amylohepatic vein on the liver surface near the medial aspect of the tumor. Partial right hemoliver mobilization begins by detaching the posterior attachment to the gyrotofascia using a hook artery. The cystic duct is clipped and divided, and the gallbladder is removed from the segment five liver bed. A prominent common baldach lymph node is excised as part of the lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon continues dissection upwards to identify the portal vein branches leading to the right hemiliver. An accessory segment six portal inflow is visible, and an artery to segment six is divided with scissors. The segment six portal vein is isolated using a Maryland robotic instrument. A six-inch silk suture is used to assist in placing Hem-o-lok clips, with two clips on the staying side and one on the specimen side before division with robotic scissors. The main right hepatic artery is dissected and isolated, with surrounding lymphatic tissue being divided. An accessory vessel to segment six is clipped and divided, and the right posterior portal vein is visible.

The surgeon divides the right posterior pulmonary vein, using ICG to identify the bile duct intrahepatically. A bulldog clamp is placed to test the inflow to the right hemoliver. The right hepatic artery is divided between clips, allowing access to the right anterior pole vein. The dissection is performed with bipolar forceps and hokari, isolating the right anterior pole vein, around which a silk suture is passed.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the right lobe of the liver, taking down attachments on the lateral aspect of the vena cava and identifying short hepatic veins. Small robotic veins are clipped carefully, and larger short hepatic veins are reinforced with figure-of-eight sutures after clipping. Traction stitches are placed on both sides of the transection plane. All inflow vessels to the right hemoliver, including the right anterior and posterior pedicles, have been ligated and divided. Monopolar cautery is used on the first centimeter of the superficial hepatic parenchyma.

The surgeon places a central line for the hepatic resection and switches to an extended vessel sealer as they progress deeper into the liver parenchyma. The assistant uses laparoscopic suction to clear smoke and irrigate with saline. The liver parenchyma is divided down to the right hepatic bile duct, which is isolated and transected using a robotic stapler. The right anterior pole vein is also transected, with the stapler visibly fired across the right hepatic bile duct.

The liver is opened in a book-like fashion as the surgeon continues dissection towards the cephalad notch. The right hepatic vein is stapled flush to the vena cava, forming a clean staple line. The hepatic segment eight parenchyma is divided bloodlessly. The large Makucha ligament is transected with a stapler, and the coronary and triangular ligaments are divided from the diaphragm. The vascularized specimen is removed using a laparoscopic catch bag through the incision at the jail point.
